title: The Break-Even Number That Quietly Changed on July 1
linkedin:
Your break-even revenue moved on 1 July. Did you recalculate it?

Award wage reviews, insurance renewals, CPI-linked rent increases and the usual software price bumps all cluster around the new financial year. None of them is dramatic on its own. Together, they quietly shift the cost base a business is pricing against.

Most owner-led businesses last worked out their break-even number properly some time in the past twelve months, then kept running on it while costs moved underneath.

The gap doesn't usually surface on a quiet Tuesday. It surfaces when a bank asks for updated year to date figures, when a business is preparing for a capital raise, or when an owner sits down to plan next year's drawings against a profit figure that isn't quite what it used to be. By then the drift has usually had two or three quarters to compound.

The fix isn't a full financial overhaul. It's pulling current fixed costs, wages, super, rent, insurance, software, dividing by the average margin across the product or service mix, and comparing that to what's actually being priced and sold today. For most businesses that's a two hour exercise once the numbers are in front of you.

What it isn't is optional. The businesses that come out of each financial year with a stronger margin are the ones that treat this as routine maintenance, not a once a year scramble.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Quick one for the July to-do list: has anyone actually recalculated your break-even number since the new financial year started?

Wage reviews, insurance renewals, rent bumps and software price increases all tend to land around now. None of them feels huge on its own, but stack them up and the number you need to hit to just cover costs has probably shifted without anyone updating the pricing to match.

We see this a lot in July. Businesses aren't doing anything wrong, they're just running on last year's maths.

Worth a proper look before it becomes a September surprise. Details on the blog.

#SMEFinance
google:
A quick prompt for business owners across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria as the new financial year settles in. If you haven't looked at your break-even revenue since 1 July, it's worth ten minutes this week.

Award wage reviews, insurance renewals, rent increases and annual software price rises all tend to cluster around the start of the financial year. Individually they're small. Together they shift the cost base your pricing was built against, often without anyone noticing until a bank, an investor or a year end review brings it to light.

The fix isn't complicated. Add up current fixed costs, divide by your average margin, and compare the result to what you're actually charging and selling today. For most owner-led businesses that's a short exercise, not a project, provided it happens now rather than in three months' time when the gap has had a chance to grow.

We work through this exact recalculation with clients every July as part of keeping their numbers current rather than reactive. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog for the detail on how the number moves and what to do once you've found it.
